summary: Today's sweep leans toward Democracy / press freedom, AI / computing, CS education / AI policy. The strongest items in this run are ABC and Disney accuse Trump admin of violating First Amendment rights, Musk v. Altman week 2: OpenAI fires back, and Shivon Zilis reveals that Musk tried to poach Sam Altman, and Sony says "efficient" AI tools will lead to even more games flooding the market.

mainItems.title: ABC and Disney accuse Trump admin of violating First Amendment rights
mainItems.summary: ABC is accusing the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) of violating its First Amendment rights by making "major shifts in policy and practice" that the network claims will chill free speech.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Musk v. Altman week 2: OpenAI fires back, and Shivon Zilis reveals that Musk tried to poach Sam Altman
mainItems.summary: In the second week of the landmark trial between Elon Musk and OpenAI, Musk’s motivations for bringing the suit were under scrutiny.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: Sony says "efficient" AI tools will lead to even more games flooding the market
mainItems.summary: But human artists still "must remain at the center," PlayStation maker says.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: California’s Education Funding Level Rises Compared to Other States
mainItems.summary: This story was originally published by EdSource. Sign up for their daily newsletter.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, student agency, and school policy.

mainItems.title: Canvas is back online, but questions — and final exam disruptions — linger
mainItems.summary: Some schools are warning users not to log back into Canvas yet, after a ransomware group claimed credit for a data breach. Half of North America's higher education institutions use the platform.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Interesting where engineering, science, and tech infrastructure collide.

mainItems.title: "They were totally game to play ball": how Zachtronics almost made a Star Trek engineering sim
mainItems.summary: This week the former Zachtronics folk of Coincidence released U.V.S. Nirmana , a new "Zach-like" puzzler that has fairly spaghettified my synapses, despite being billed as "medium-difficulty".
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Trump’s looming defeat in Iran is a personal and political crisis | Robert Reich
mainItems.summary: Trump’s ego cannot accept a humiliating loss, and we are already seeing the effects of his failure playing out We are witnessing what happens to a person who is consumed with the need to dominate, but cannot. Iran is unlikely to give in.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Keir Starmer under pressure to agree exit plan after election mauling
mainItems.summary: Senior Labour MPs urge prime minister to step down within year as party loses control of 25 English councils and humbled in Wales Full results from England, Scotland and Wales Mapped: how Labour lost ground in different directions Keir…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: Family Reunion is a dinnertime simulation game that captures the wonder and boredom of being a loosely disciplined 7-year-old
mainItems.summary: I first played Family Reunion at Gamescom Latam last week, which in hindsight was a bit of trek, considering the demo is right there on Itch and Steam .
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth your time if you care about design, criticism, and not just hype.

mainItems.title: 'The Biggest Student Data Privacy Disaster in History': Canvas Hack Shows the Danger of Centralized EdTech
mainItems.summary: Messages could include "medical circumstances, accessibility accommodations, disputes, sexual assault allegations," and more.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

mainItems.title: Lack of U.S. response to hantavirus outbreak worries public health experts
mainItems.summary: Public health experts are raising concerns about why the U.S. government hasn't had a more public response to the hantavirus outbreak that started on a cruise ship.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

mainItems.title: Hawaiʻi Families Need Preschool. Who Will Fund It?
mainItems.summary: Affordable preschool options are few and far between for Hannah Miller, a Waikōloa mom of a 2- and 4-year-old. For more than three years, Miller has relied on a free early learning program run out of a church in Waimea.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, classroom practice, and educational judgment.

mainItems.title: What to Watch and Read This Weekend: Do Astronauts Dream of Sheep Detectives?
mainItems.summary: Plus: David Attenborough and Victor LaValle The post What to Watch and Read This Weekend: Do Astronauts Dream of Sheep Detectives? appeared first on Reactor .
mainItems.whyItMatters: On-theme for your cultural interests, especially when the concept is strong.

new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

new.title: Constellation
new.why: A little more cerebral and mood-heavy, but it scratches the identity / reality-slippage itch.

new.title: The Devil’s Hour
new.why: Dark, twisty, and structurally ambitious without feeling like homework.

older.title: Counterpart
older.why: Probably the single cleanest “James show you may have missed”: espionage + parallel-world sci-fi + adult competence.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Utopia
older.why: Paranoid conspiracy energy, formal boldness, and a willingness to get strange and ugly.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

older.title: Continuum
older.why: A cleaner old-school sci-fi fit if you want procedural momentum wrapped around time-travel politics.

older.title: Person of Interest
older.why: Starts procedural and then quietly becomes one of the smartest AI / surveillance shows on television.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.

highlights.title: Solve a murder mystery by flying people around on your giant crane in the Disco Elysium and Chinese folktale inspired The Crane Rider's Tale
highlights.notes: Pinned from today's briefing at James's request.

highlights.title: 12 unexpected sourdough bread recipes that aren't crusty loaves: From sourdough flatbread to sourdough milk bread — and even sourdough babka.
highlights.notes: Pinned from today's briefing, the kind of bread-nerd rabbit hole worth keeping.

highlights.title: A.I. Is Prompting an Evolution, Not Extinction, for Coders
highlights.notes: Interesting piece on the current state of AI in software work.

highlights.title: The Workers Letting A.I. Do Their Jobs
highlights.notes: In an era of agents powered by artificial intelligence, many programmers are barely programming.
